perturbation.original_text: Each Party, for itself and its associates as defined below, represents and warrants that it shall not conduct business with any sources or contacts, or said source's or contact's associates as defined below, that are originally made known and/or available by another Party hereto, at any time or in any manner, without the express written permission (not to be unreasonably withheld) of the Party who made the source(s) known and/or available.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party, for itself and its associates as defined below, will endeavor not to conduct business with any sources or contacts, or said source's or contact's associates as defined below, that are originally made known and/or available by another Party hereto, at any time or in any manner, unless it is deemed commercially unreasonable to obtain written permission from the Party who made the source(s) known and/or available.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not conduct business' to 'will endeavor not to conduct business' weakens the prohibition. Adding 'unless it is deemed commercially unreasonable' introduces a subjective standard.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA aims to protect trade secrets. By allowing a party to conduct business with another party's contacts if it is 'deemed commercially unreasonable' to obtain permission, the modification weakens the protection of potentially confidential business contacts revealed under the agreement, thus undermining PUTSA's objective to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5304 (2022) - Damages :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5304 - Damages Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5304 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5304. Damages. (a) Monetary damages. --Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) Exemplary damages. --If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a). perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall not conduct business' to 'will endeavor not to conduct business' and the addition of 'unless it is deemed commercially unreasonable' weakens the obligation. While the provided law snippet, 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5304 (2022), focuses on damages for misappropriation of trade secrets, the Law Explanation states that the original contract aims to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets by protecting confidential business contacts. The modified language introduces ambiguity and weakens the protection of potentially confidential contacts, contradicting the purpose described in the Law Explanation, which is to prevent misappropriation, thus undermining the principles of trade secret protection. The modified text makes the agreement more difficult to enforce, as 'endeavor' and 'commercially unreasonable' are subjective and could be easily challenged, making it easier to circumvent the original intent to protect the trade secret.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties will maintain complete confidentiality regarding this Agreement and all transactions occurring thereunder, each other's business, business sources and affiliates and each other's propriety knowledge and know-how, and will disclose such information only pursuant to the express written permission of the party who made such information available save where such information deemed to be in the public domain or under the order of a competent Court or Government Agency.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties will endeavor to maintain confidentiality regarding this Agreement and transactions, and each other's business, sources and affiliates and each other's knowledge and know-how, and may disclose such information with reasonable justification, save where such information is clearly in the public domain or under the incontestable order of a Court or Government Agency.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will maintain complete confidentiality' with 'will endeavor to maintain confidentiality' reduces the obligation's stringency. 'Reasonable justification' replaces express written permission, making disclosure easier to justify. 'Clearly in the public domain' introduces subjectivity, and incontestable order could create unnecessary hurdles in complying with legally valid orders.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA defines misappropriation of trade secrets to include unauthorized disclosure. By allowing disclosure with 'reasonable justification' instead of 'express written permission', the modification makes it harder to prove misappropriation because 'reasonable justification' is vague, increasing risk that protected trade secrets will be disclosed.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5302 (2022) - Definitions :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5302 - Definitions Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5302 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5302. Definitions. The following words and phrases when used in this chapter shall have the meanings given to them in this section unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: "Improper means." Includes, but is not limited to, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation." Includes: (1) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person." A natural person, corporation,
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity and weakens confidentiality obligations, potentially conflicting with trade secret protection laws. The original text requires 'complete confidentiality' and disclosure only with 'express written permission.' The changed text replaces this with 'endeavor to maintain confidentiality' and allows disclosure with 'reasonable justification.' This shift undermines the protection of trade secrets. Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5302 defines 'misappropriation' to include 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' The 'reasonable justification' standard in the modified text introduces a subjective element that makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation, as it broadens the scope for permissible disclosure beyond 'express written permission.' This creates a potential legal contradiction by weakening the obligation to maintain secrecy, as highlighted in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party represents, warrants and covenants that all information furnished by said party, or to be furnished by said Party, or to any other Party or Parties hereto is, or will be, true, complete, correct and accurate to best of said Party's knowledge, ability and belief.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party represents that all information furnished by said party, or to be furnished by said Party, is believed to be reasonably accurate and complete based on available information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the warranty and covenant weakens the assurance. Replacing 'true, complete, correct and accurate to best of said Party's knowledge, ability and belief' with 'believed to be reasonably accurate and complete based on available information' significantly reduces the standard of accuracy.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 401 of the Pennsylvania Securities Act prohibits making untrue statements of material fact or omitting material facts necessary to make statements not misleading in connection with the offer, sale, or purchase of any security. By only representing the information is 'believed to be reasonably accurate and complete based on available information', the modified language creates a lower standard than what's required by the Act, which requires completeness and accuracy regarding material facts related to a security.
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a warranty of 'true, complete, correct and accurate to best of said Party's knowledge, ability and belief' with a mere representation that information is 'believed to be reasonably accurate and complete based on available information.' According to the law explanation, this creates a lower standard than the Pennsylvania Securities Act, which requires completeness and accuracy regarding material facts related to a security, because it only requires a reasonable belief in accuracy. This alteration weakens the legal obligation to provide accurate and complete information, creating ambiguity around the level of assurance required. Since there is no scraped law snippet, the determination is based only on the legal and perturbation explanations.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of circumvention by the 'Parties' involved in this transaction, either directly or indirectly, it is agreed and guaranteed that a monetary penalty will be paid by the person or persons engaged in or circumvention. This payment will additionally include all reasonable legal expenses incurred by the aggrieved party.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of possible circumvention by the 'Parties' involved in this transaction, either directly or indirectly, it is suggested that a mutually agreed upon penalty may be considered. Reimbursement of legal expenses will be determined on a case-by-case basis.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'it is agreed and guaranteed that a monetary penalty will be paid' with 'it is suggested that a mutually agreed upon penalty may be considered' makes the penalty discretionary and dependent on mutual agreement. Changing 'will additionally include all reasonable legal expenses' to 'will be determined on a case-by-case basis' introduces uncertainty regarding legal expense reimbursement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania contract law regarding remedies for breach of contract
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania contract law, following the Restatement (Second) of Contracts, allows for expectation damages and other remedies to compensate a party for breach of contract. By making the penalty for circumvention discretionary and reimbursement of legal fees uncertain, the modified language weakens the remedies available to the aggrieved party. Expectation damages are intended to put the injured party in as good a position as if the contract had been fully performed. Note that a direct link to Restatement Second of Contracts is not available as these restatements are sold in libraries. We used a link to law.justia.com, but that directed to 14th Amendment of the U.S. Constitution. The Second URL leads to Lexis, and it is a more reliable link to use in this case to review the law.

Wex commercial law criminal law wex definitions In civil cases: A remedy associated with [wex:unjust enrichment] in which the amount of recovery is typically based on the [wex:defendant]'s gain rather than the [wex:plaintiff]'s loss. In criminal cases: Full or partial compensation for loss paid by a criminal to a victim that is ordered as part of a criminal [wex:sentence] or as a condition of [wex:probation]. Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Restitution - Definition, Examples, Cases Restitution February 6, 2015 by: Content Team In law, restitution is often ordered by a court in order to achieve fairness, preventing the unjust enrichment of one party to a civil lawsuit . In addition, restitution is often ordered in criminal sentencing, requiring the defendant to make monetary amends, or perform some act that benefits the victim of the crime, or the public in general. Restitution in a criminal setting is usually made in addition to fines and/or jail time. To explore this concept, consider the following restitution definition. Definition of Restitution Noun Payment made to someone, or the giving of an equivalent value, to compensate them for damages or loss. Origin 1350-1400 Middle English restitucioun Restitution in a Civil Lawsuit An order of restitution in a civil lawsuit is referred to as “civil damages.” How the amount of restitution (“damages”) to be made is calculated varies by the type of case. In a contract case, damages are awarded in the amount of the contract, or in the amount required to return the prevailing party to his financial position prior to the incident. This is referred to as making the party “whole.” In a personal injury case, the injured party may be awarded damages in the amount of his medical bills, an amount to cover damages to his personal property if applicable, and he may be awarded a monetary amount for “pain and suffering.” Example of Restitution in a Contract Case Amelia and Bob hire professional photographer Dan to take pictures at their wedding, specifying that, in addition to photographing the wedding ceremony, photos are to be taken of their families and the reception. Amelia wrote a check for Dan’s entire $5,000 fee when they hired him. Dan delivers a wedding album to the couple, explaining that there had been an electronic malfunction of the camera after the ceremony, and none of the family or reception photos turned out. Dan offers Amelia and Bob a partial refund of $200, which they refuse, feeling they deserve the entire amount back, as he ruined their wedding memories. When the parties meet in civil court, the judge determines that, although it was through no fault of his own, Dan did not provide the couple with adequate value for the contract. The judge also determines that the couple did receive partial value, and so orders the photographer to refund half of his fee, $2,500. Example of Restitution in a Personal Injury Case As Jennifer slows to a stop at a red light, another car, driven by Amanda, slams into her from the rear, shoving her car into the car ahead of her. Jennifer’s small car is totaled, and Jennifer sustains a broken leg and several lacerations. Jennifer’s medical bills amount to $6,000, and she misses three days of work. Jennifer sues for medical bills, $10,000 for her totaled car, wages for the days she missed work, and pain and suffering. If Jennifer proves that Amanda was at fault for the accident in civil court, she will be awarded the full amount of her medical bills, and may be awarded the actual amount of her wages for the missed days, which is $240. The judge finds, however, that Jennifer’s 10-year old car is only worth $4,000, even though she paid $10,000 for it two years ago. Pain and suffering is awarded and calculated at the judge’s discretion, but is generally equal to between 1.5 and 3 times the total medical bills. As a result, Amanda is ordered to pay a total of $28,240 to Jennifer. Description Amount Medical Bills $6,000 Lost Wages $240 Property Damage (car) $4,000 Pain and Suffering $18,000 TOTAL $25,240 Paying Restitution in a Criminal Case An order for paying restitution in a criminal matter is often fashioned in accordance with the crime committed. For example, if the defendant has been convicted of stealing tools out of someone’s garage, he may be ordered to reimburse the victim for the value of the tools. On the other hand, if the defendant is convicted of harassing homeless people dow
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates a monetary penalty for circumvention and guarantees reimbursement of legal expenses, ensuring the aggrieved party is made whole. The changed text introduces ambiguity by suggesting a 'mutually agreed upon penalty' and determining legal expense reimbursement on a 'case-by-case basis.' This contradicts the principle of restitution, which aims to compensate for loss or injury done and prevent unjust enrichment, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1. Snippet 2 supports this further by highlighting that in contract cases, damages are awarded to return the prevailing party to their financial position prior to the incident which ensures the party is 'whole.' The modified language weakens these protections, making compensation uncertain and discretionary.
